text: The surgeon uses suction to gently dissect and separate the omentum, revealing pus flakes and a distended gallbladder with gangrenous changes. Inflammatory adhesions are removed with suction, and peri-hepatic and sub-diaphragmatic fluid collections are aspirated. The gallbladder is separated from the omentum and colon, and a 5mm working port is inserted at the mid-clavicular line and anterior axillary line for gallbladder retraction. The gallbladder is pushed back using suction, assisted by tooth forceps.

The surgeon uses suction and a hook to dissect the gallbladder, which is grasped by the assistant and pushed back. Due to the thickness and edema, the gallbladder is punctured at the gangrenous site, allowing for complete aspiration of pus. The left hand compresses the duodenum and colon momentum while the right hand performs gentle suction work.

The surgeon uses gentle suction to create a posterior window and lift the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed. The dissection of Calot's triangle is performed using sweeping movements with the suction device, revealing the cystic artery. Care is taken to avoid excessive traction to prevent vessel avulsion and bleeding.

The surgeon delineates Calot's triangle, revealing two tubular structures entering the gallbladder. Two-thirds of the gallbladder is lifted from the gallbladder bed. A clip is applied to the cystic artery, followed by the cystic duct, which is lifted and defined completely. Suction is used gently to maintain a clear surgical field and open edematous planes.

The surgeon compresses the bulging omentum using an assistant port on the left side to gain complete access. The cystic duct is cut, and the gallbladder, already punctured, is lifted from the gallbladder bed using suction. Dissection continues at the fundus with a hook, managing minor bleeding due to inflammation with compression and cautery.

The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder specimen using a glove bag through the epigastric port. Clips are visible on the cystic artery and duct, with a clean surgical field showing no bleeding from the gallbladder bed. The colon appears grossly dilated due to gallbladder inflammation. A thorough lavage is performed in the right upper quadrant and pelvis using suction through the umbilical port. A drain is placed in the subhepatic space near the gallbladder fossa through the right-sided port.
